## [00:00 - 03:03]

Okay, the service customer. Customers that are currently getting service with your firm whether you sold them or not, doesn't matter. They're getting service, repairs, whatever. There's a great opportunity that sales people tend to see service customers as service customers rather than how I see them, future prospects. The customer that came in for a watch repair. I brought the watch in the other day. I want a new band on it, right? First person sees me as a band buyer, rather than a watch buyer. How do you think I got the freaking watch in my arm, dude? The customer that comes in for a watch repair is a watch buyer, but nobody ever contacts him from the sales department. Same with the customer calling for a tailor in the men's department. That guy's a buyer for another suit. Why didn't somebody in the sales department talk to the guy that wants to get his pants taken up? The customer complaint at the hardware store. They'll all change at the car dealership. These are customers. How about the customer calling the bank saying, I can't access my account online? That guy's a prospect for other bank products, but nobody ever does that. The guy can go on and on. All businesses fail here with service customers. Businesses don't think like that because they're too dependent upon mass advertising and marketing. See prospecting got lost as a priority when we went into the information age and telecommunications and TVs in every household. And billboards, see everyone's a prospect. And people that are servicing their products that you sell, they're as close to your product and loyal to your company as you can get. These people are having service problems, questions on their products. They're warm. They're warm. They're hot. They're like 211 degrees. All we need to do is add some intention and it hits 212. These are great prospects for what you offer. This to me is an immediate source of great contacts for me to meet with, get out of obscurity with, get attention of. All problems from clients that have bought our products, all problems are opportunities to solve problems and create new clients. A lot of salespeople are like, well, I don't want to talk to the repair order because he's going to be a problem. I don't want to talk to the guy having a problem with this product because, oh my gosh, he's going to consume me. Those are opportunities. See poor people don't want problems. Rich people look for them. Where's the problem at? I need a problem. I need to find a problem. I got to solve a problem because they know money follows solutions. Each day I'm going to get a copy. This is what I'm going to do. I'm going to get a copy of every service invoice or repair order for our company. I'd love to get a list of complaints. Let's just keep it with service invoices and repair orders so that I can use these to build up my database, my contact, my power base. I'm going to even intercept possibly service calls. I might tell the receptionist, hey, anything going to service route it to me.

## [03:03 - 04:28]

I'll schedule them. See, when I sell a customer, I want to tell them, look, anything you have, you got a band goes front. You lose a pin. You got anything. You want to clean the watch. You don't need to buy a cleaning fluid here. Come see me. I'll clean it every day, every third day, every week. Why would I want to do that? Because I want to stay in contact with my customer. I want to control the scheduling of service for them because I know a service customer is an opportunity for me to stay in touch with them, for me to keep my name in front of them, and for me to even possibly resell them. Customer comes in with a repair for the band. On their watch. And if I'm unable to intercept and the service customer goes through the normal service channels, I'm going to later call them back. My preference again is to intercept them. But if I can't, I'm going to make follow up a service call a priority. To be sure they were handled correctly, got handled. Their problem got handled again. What am I trying to do? Get out of this obscurity. Get attention. Be of service. Make a contact. Action. Here's the action you're going to take. One, make servicing customers a target priority. Number two, figure out how you can intercept the information. Number three, start building a file for just service and repairs. Four, make contact. Intercept if you can. And call them if you can't.
